skill.name: vr-ar
skill.description: VR/AR development principles. Comfort, interaction, performance requirements.
skill.summary: Expert guidance for building comfortable VR/AR experiences with proper performance optimization and interaction design.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static scanner flagged 7 potential issues (4 weak cryptography, 3 system reconnaissance) but all are FALSE POSITIVES after review. This is a documentation-only skill with no executable code. Pattern matching errors on legitimate platform names (ARCore, ARKit) and terminology (camera shake). No security risks detected.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Platform Name Pattern Match
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'ARCore' and 'ARKit' platform names as potential weak cryptographic algorithms. These are legitimate AR framework names, not cryptographic implementations. No security risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Clear documentation of AR platforms - ARCore/ARKit are industry-standard frameworks. No cryptographic code present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Camera Shake Terminology
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'camera shake' as system reconnaissance. This refers to VR motion sickness prevention techniques, not system commands. No reconnaissance activity.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Context is clearly about VR comfort settings and motion sickness prevention, not system exploration.

content.value_statement: Create immersive VR and AR applications that prevent motion sickness and maintain optimal performance. Learn industry-tested principles for comfortable spatial computing design.
actual_capabilities: Provides VR/AR platform comparison tables for device selection
Explains motion sickness prevention techniques with specific solutions
Lists performance requirements and frame budgets for major VR platforms
Documents interaction patterns for controllers and hand tracking
Defines spatial design principles including world scale and depth cues
Identifies common anti-patterns to avoid in VR/AR development
limitations: Documentation-only skill with no code execution capabilities
Does not generate code or implement VR/AR features automatically
Platform-specific details may change as hardware evolves
Performance targets are general guidelines that vary by application complexity

use_cases.title: New VR Developer Learning
use_cases.description: Beginner VR developers learn fundamental comfort principles and performance targets before building their first immersive experience.
use_cases.target_user: Indie game developer exploring VR for the first time

use_cases.title: AR Platform Selection
use_cases.description: Product teams compare ARKit, ARCore, WebXR, and HoloLens platforms to choose the right technology stack for their mobile AR application.
use_cases.target_user: Technical lead evaluating AR platforms for enterprise app

use_cases.title: VR Performance Optimization
use_cases.description: Experienced developers reference frame budgets and optimization techniques to maintain 90 FPS on Quest 2/3 and prevent motion discomfort.
use_cases.target_user: VR developer optimizing existing application for Quest platform

prompt_templates.title: Platform Selection Guidance
prompt_templates.prompt: Which VR platform should I choose for a standalone fitness game with multiplayer features? Compare Quest vs PCVR trade-offs for my use case.
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer choosing between standalone and tethered VR platforms

prompt_templates.title: Motion Sickness Prevention
prompt_templates.prompt: Players report nausea during fast movement sections. Review comfort principles and suggest specific locomotion and camera techniques to reduce motion sickness.
prompt_templates.scenario: Troubleshooting player comfort issues in VR game

prompt_templates.title: Performance Optimization Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: My Quest 2 app drops to 60 FPS in complex scenes. Analyze the frame budget breakdown and prioritize optimization techniques to maintain consistent 90 FPS.
prompt_templates.scenario: Optimizing VR application for consistent frame rate

prompt_templates.title: Spatial Design Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Design interaction patterns for a VR painting app using hand tracking. Apply controller interaction principles and depth cues for immersive experience.
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating natural hand-tracking interactions for VR application

output_examples.input: Which VR platform should I target for a high-fidelity single-player narrative game?
output_examples.output: For a high-fidelity narrative game, PCVR is recommended because it supports higher resolution (2160x2160+ per eye) and greater graphical complexity than standalone Quest. Consider a Quest port later using adaptive quality settings to maintain the 90 FPS target. The key trade-off is PCVR requires a tethered connection and gaming PC, while Quest offers wireless convenience but limited graphics processing power.

output_examples.input: Why do players get sick in my VR game during movement?
output_examples.output: Motion sickness typically occurs from three causes in your description: 1) Camera movement without player control (vestibular mismatch), 2) Frame drops below 90 FPS causing visual judder, and 3) Rapid acceleration changes. Implement teleportation or snap-turn locomotion options, add a vignette effect during movement, and profile your frame times to identify performance bottlenecks. Test with comfort-sensitive users and provide multiple comfort settings in your options menu.

output_examples.input: How do I scale objects correctly in VR?
output_examples.output: World scale is critical in VR: set your 3D engine so 1 unit equals exactly 1 meter. This ensures objects appear at natural sizes. Test by placing a 1.8m cube and verifying it feels like human height when standing next to it. For UI elements, place them 2-3 meters away at comfortable reading angles, and ensure interactive objects are within 0.5-1.5 meters for natural arm reach. Incorrect scale breaks presence and causes discomfort.

best_practices: Maintain consistent 90 FPS on Quest 2 and 90-120 FPS on Quest 3 to prevent motion discomfort and judder
Provide multiple comfort options including teleportation, snap-turning, and vignette effects to accommodate different sensitivity levels
Test VR experiences with comfort-sensitive users early in development, as developers often build tolerance to motion that affects new players
Design UI text at minimum 20-30 pixels tall and place within 2-3 meters for readability across all VR platforms
anti_patterns: Moving the camera without explicit player input causes immediate vestibular mismatch and motion sickness in most users
Dropping below platform frame rate targets (90 FPS for PCVR, 72-90 FPS for Quest 2) creates visible judder that breaks presence and discomfort
Using desktop-sized UI text in VR makes interface elements unreadable; VR requires 2-3x larger text for legibility
Ignoring world scale (1 unit = 1 meter) makes objects feel unnaturally sized and breaks spatial presence

faq.question: What FPS do I need to target for VR?
faq.answer: Target 90 FPS minimum for PCVR and PSVR2, 72-90 FPS for Quest 2, and 90-120 FPS for Quest 3. Consistent frame timing is more important than peak FPS. A single dropped frame causes visible judder. Profile your application using platform tools to identify rendering bottlenecks.

faq.question: Should I build for Quest or PCVR?
faq.answer: Choose Quest for standalone wireless experiences with broader audience reach. Choose PCVR for high-fidelity graphics requiring gaming PC hardware. WebXR works for browser-based access across devices. Consider developing for PCVR first with graphical fidelity, then optimize for Quest using adaptive resolution and LOD systems.

faq.question: How do I prevent motion sickness in VR?
faq.answer: Implement player-controlled locomotion (teleportation, snap turns) rather than forced camera movement. Add comfort vignettes during motion. Maintain consistent frame rate above platform minimums. Avoid rapid acceleration and camera shake. Always provide multiple comfort options in settings so users can customize their experience.

faq.question: What is the difference between VR and AR development?
faq.answer: VR fully immerses users in virtual environments requiring high frame rates and comfort considerations. AR overlays digital content on the real world using device cameras (ARKit/ARCore) and requires different approaches to occlusion, lighting, and environmental understanding. Performance constraints differ significantly between tethered PCVR, standalone Quest, and mobile AR.

faq.question: How do I design UI for VR/AR?
faq.answer: Place UI elements 2-3 meters away in VR with large text (minimum 20-30 pixels tall). Use gaze, raycast, or controller pointing for interaction rather than head gaze alone. For AR, anchor UI to real-world surfaces and ensure readability in varied lighting conditions. Test text legibility with multiple users across different platforms.

faq.question: Can this skill generate VR/AR code for me?
faq.answer: No, this skill provides design principles and best practices documentation. It cannot automatically generate code or implement features. Use the guidance to inform your development decisions in Unity, Unreal, or WebXR frameworks. Claude can help apply these principles when writing specific code, but the skill itself is reference material.
